topics.brief: A developer-friendly explainer of zero-knowledge encryption. How BitAtlas encrypts files in your browser before upload using AES-256-GCM, PBKDF2 key derivation, and per-file random keys. Why the server literally cannot see your data.

AI agents are reading and writing files on your behalf. But where do those files live? Why agents need a dedicated encrypted vault — not just a folder on your desktop. How BitAtlas's MCP server gives agents secure, scoped file access.

Germany mandated ODF and PDF/UA for all public administration. What this means for digital sovereignty, vendor lock-in, and why BitAtlas is built on the same principles of open, vendor-neutral infrastructure.

Dropbox, Google Drive, and OneDrive can all read your files. A comparison of mainstream cloud storage vs zero-knowledge alternatives like BitAtlas, Tresorit, and pCloud Crypto. What 'encryption at rest' really means vs true zero-knowledge.

A technical walkthrough of how we built the BitAtlas MCP server. 7 tools, client-side AES-256-GCM, presigned S3 uploads, and how any AI agent can interact with an encrypted vault via the Model Context Protocol.

A deep-dive into implementing client-side encryption using the Web Crypto API. How BitAtlas derives keys with PBKDF2, encrypts files with AES-256-GCM, and wraps per-file keys with the master key — all in the browser.
Why hosting your data on European-owned infrastructure (not just an EU region of AWS) matters. The US CLOUD Act, GDPR, and why BitAtlas chose Hetzner over hyperscalers.

The tradeoff every zero-knowledge service faces: if you lose your password, your data is gone. Why this is a feature, not a bug. How PBKDF2 key derivation works and why we refuse to add a 'reset password' backdoor.

How BitAtlas uploads files without the server ever touching them. The presigned URL pattern: client encrypts → gets a signed URL → uploads directly to S3/MinIO. The server only stores metadata and encrypted keys.

How to give AI agents access to your vault without giving them your password. BitAtlas API keys, permission scoping, and why the agent needs a pre-derived master key (not your password) for encryption.

The intersection of digital legacy planning and AI agent infrastructure. How LegacyShield uses BitAtlas as its storage layer, and why the future of estate planning is zero-knowledge + agent-accessible.

keywords: encryption at rest vs zero knowledge
topics.brief: Most cloud providers encrypt your data 'at rest' — but they hold the keys. That's like a hotel safe where the front desk has a master key. The difference between encryption at rest, in transit, and true zero-knowledge.
For the privacy maximalists: how to self-host your own BitAtlas-style encrypted storage using MinIO, Node.js, and the Web Crypto API. A practical guide to building your own zero-knowledge vault.
How zero-knowledge encryption makes GDPR compliance simpler. When you delete your account, the encrypted blobs become permanently unreadable — because the key is gone. True erasure by design.
The state of the MCP ecosystem in 2026. How many servers exist, what categories dominate, and where encrypted storage fits in. A survey of Smithery, Glama, awesome-mcp-servers, and the registries.
Encrypting a 5GB file in a browser tab is hard. We break down chunking strategies, readable streams, and using IndexedDB as a buffer to ensure smooth user experience without crashing the main thread.
Beyond passwords: using RSA or Ed25519 for key wrapping. How BitAtlas allows for shared vaults by encrypting per-file AES keys with the recipient's public key.
What if you lose your master key? Exploring Shamir's Secret Sharing (SSS) to split your master key among trusted friends or multiple devices, ensuring recovery without a central authority.

Is your data safe from future quantum computers? A look at NIST's post-quantum standards (Kyber, Dilithium) and how they can be implemented in the browser today to protect against 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks.

How Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP) can be used to verify that a server is actually storing your data correctly without the server ever knowing what the data is. The intersection of BitAtlas and verifiable computing.
Data residency is where it sits; data sovereignty is who has jurisdiction. Why BitAtlas prioritizes both by using EU-owned infrastructure and client-side encryption to remove jurisdictional risk.
Using Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) like Intel SGX or AWS Nitro Enclaves to run AI agents that process BitAtlas data without the host provider being able to peek inside the RAM.
A checklist for MCP server developers: how to handle API keys, how to scope tool permissions, and why you should never pass raw user credentials through an LLM.

Can you have Google Docs-style collaboration with zero-knowledge encryption? Exploring CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) like Yjs and Automerge with client-side encryption for secure team work.
Implementing 'burn-after-reading' logic in BitAtlas. How we can use time-limited presigned URLs and client-side key deletion to create vaults that literally disappear.
How do you know BitAtlas is actually doing what it says? A guide for security researchers to audit our open-source client-side code and verify the encryption implementation.
The trend towards developers owning their entire stack — from hardware to encryption. How tools like BitAtlas, Nix, and Tailscale are enabling a new era of infrastructure independence.
Using S3 Object Lock and zero-knowledge encryption to create a backup strategy that is immune to ransomware. Even if your master key is stolen, the attacker can't delete your old versions.
Why BitAtlas doesn't need to know who you are to store your data. Exploring decentralized identifiers (DIDs) and how we can decouple your identity from your encrypted blobs.
